review_reasons.message: the pay stubs report different gross totals; the recurring base is taken from the stub that reconciles with its own hours and rate, and the excess is not annualized (stub totals ['2,166.00', '2,500.00']; using 2,166.00 from HH-001-D03, which reconciles with its own hours and rate; HH-001-D02 (2,500.00) was not used as the recurring base because its own regular_hours * hourly_rate is 76 * 28.5 = 2,166.00)

a value the renter corrected was NOT used as the recurring base, because after the correction that document no longer settles what the recurring pay is; the annualized amount does not reflect the correction (gross_pay on HH-001-D02 was corrected to 2,500.00, but its own regular_hours * hourly_rate is 76 * 28.5 = 2,166.00 on that same document; the recurring base was taken from HH-001-D03 instead, so the corrected figure does not change the annualized amount)

citations.text: Return READY_TO_REVIEW only when required evidence is present, current under the challenge's 60-day convention, internally consistent, and traceable to page-level source boxes. Otherwise return NEEDS_REVIEW with reasons.
For scoring only, annualize recurring gross income using the explicit pay frequency. Sum independently documented recurring sources. Do not infer protected traits or undocumented income.

Outputs may compare an annualized amount with a frozen threshold, but must not label a person eligible, ineligible, approved, denied, or prioritized. Final determinations remain human and program-specific.

For the Boston-Cambridge-Quincy, MA-NH HMFA, the FY 2026 median family income is $164,600 and the 60% limits for household sizes 1-8 are 72,000; 82,320; 92,580; 102,840; 111,120; 119,340; 127,560; and 135,780 dollars.
FY 2026 Multifamily Tax Subsidy Project income limits are effective May 1, 2026.

abstentions.reason: the pay stubs report different gross totals; the recurring base is taken from the stub that reconciles with its own hours and rate, and the excess is not annualized (stub totals ['2,166.00', '2,500.00']; using 2,166.00 from HH-001-D03, which reconciles with its own hours and rate; HH-001-D02 (2,500.00) was not used as the recurring base because its own regular_hours * hourly_rate is 76 * 28.5 = 2,166.00)
abstentions.what_would_resolve_it: the renter or employer confirms whether the extra pay recurs

abstentions.reason: a value the renter corrected was NOT used as the recurring base, because after the correction that document no longer settles what the recurring pay is; the annualized amount does not reflect the correction (gross_pay on HH-001-D02 was corrected to 2,500.00, but its own regular_hours * hourly_rate is 76 * 28.5 = 2,166.00 on that same document; the recurring base was taken from HH-001-D03 instead, so the corrected figure does not change the annualized amount)
abstentions.what_would_resolve_it: the renter also corrects regular_hours or hourly_rate on that document so the three figures agree, or the renter or employer confirms which document reflects recurring pay

human_decision_notice: This is not an eligibility determination. A qualified housing professional must decide.

messages.headline: Your pay stubs show different totals
messages.body: One stub shows $2,166 and another shows $2,500. We used $2,166 as your regular pay, because the hours and the hourly rate on that stub add up to it, and we treated the difference as extra pay for one period rather than counting it across the year. If that extra pay comes every time, your yearly figure would be higher.
messages.action: Ask your employer whether the extra pay is a regular part of your wages, then tell us what they say.
messages.detail: the pay stubs report different gross totals; the recurring base is taken from the stub that reconciles with its own hours and rate, and the excess is not annualized (stub totals ['2,166.00', '2,500.00']; using 2,166.00 from HH-001-D03, which reconciles with its own hours and rate; HH-001-D02 (2,500.00) was not used as the recurring base because its own regular_hours * hourly_rate is 76 * 28.5 = 2,166.00)
messages.precision_note: We say the extra pay was 'not counted across the whole year' rather than 'ignored'. It was neither ignored nor annualized: it is reported and set aside. Calling it ignored would understate what we did with it.

messages.headline: Check the pay stub dated 27 June 2026
messages.body: You changed the total pay on this stub to $2,500, but its own hours and hourly rate, 76 hours at $28.50 an hour, come to $2,166. Because the two do not match, we left this stub out when we worked out your yearly income. Your change is saved, and a housing worker can see it.
messages.action: Tell us which amount is right, or add a stub that shows your usual pay. If the hours or the hourly rate are also wrong, correct those too.
messages.detail: a value the renter corrected was NOT used as the recurring base, because after the correction that document no longer settles what the recurring pay is; the annualized amount does not reflect the correction (gross_pay on HH-001-D02 was corrected to 2,500.00, but its own regular_hours * hourly_rate is 76 * 28.5 = 2,166.00 on that same document; the recurring base was taken from HH-001-D03 instead, so the corrected figure does not change the annualized amount)
messages.precision_note: We say 'we left this stub out', not 'we ignored your change'. The change is stored and shown to the reviewer; what it did not do is move the total. Saying we ignored it would be false, and saying it was applied would be worse.

CHK-APPLICATION-SUMMARY.basis: A paper counts as recent only if it is dated 19 May 2026 or later. That is the 60-day rule this project follows, counting back from 18 July 2026.

plain.reading_note: Plain wording sits on top of the precise wording; it never replaces it. Each item carries the original machine code and message so a reviewer can check that the meaning survived the rewrite.

status.headline: Your file needs a few things before a person can read it
status.body: Some papers are missing, out of date, or do not agree with each other, and we list each one below with what to do about it. Every one you clear now is one a housing worker will not have to send your file back for. None of this is a finding about you. It is about the paperwork, and paperwork you can fix.
status.action: Work through your list below. Each item says exactly what to send or who to ask.
status.detail: NEEDS_REVIEW: one or more of the four CH-READINESS-001 conditions is unmet; reasons are enumerated in review_reasons[].
status.precision_note: 'Needs a few things' describes the packet. It must not be read as a judgement about the person, which is why the body says so outright.

plain.screen_text: Your file needs a few things before a person can read it. Some papers are missing, out of date, or do not agree with each other, and we list each one below with what to do about it. Every one you clear now is one a housing worker will not have to send your file back for. None of this is a finding about you. It is about the paperwork, and paperwork you can fix. Work through your list below. Each item says exactly what to send or who to ask. Your pay stubs show different totals. One stub shows $2,166 and another shows $2,500. We used $2,166 as your regular pay, because the hours and the hourly rate on that stub add up to it, and we treated the difference as extra pay for one period rather than counting it across the year. If that extra pay comes every time, your yearly figure would be higher. Ask your employer whether the extra pay is a regular part of your wages, then tell us what they say. Check the pay stub dated 27 June 2026. You changed the total pay on this stub to $2,500, but its own hours and hourly rate, 76 hours at $28.50 an hour, come to $2,166. Because the two do not match, we left this stub out when we worked out your yearly income. Your change is saved, and a housing worker can see it. Tell us which amount is right, or add a stub that shows your usual pay. If the hours or the hourly rate are also wrong, correct those too.

activity_log.notice: Actions only. This log never holds the contents of a document, a file name, or a value you typed.
